original_abstract:
Gender equality reforms within parliamentary institutions have grown increasingly diverse and complex. In this thematic issue, we explore the shifting landscape of making parliaments more gender-sensitive, moving beyond traditional metrics of descriptive representation to examine how institutions prioritize equality in their internal cultures and daily operations. We trace these developments through a dual lens: the top-down efforts of international organizations and the crucial bottom-up pressure exerted by grassroots movements.

By leveraging feminist institutionalism, we analyze the intricate relationships between descriptive, substantive, and symbolic representation in diverse political settings. This collection bridges the gap between the Global North and Global South, highlighting "extraordinary cases"—from the gender-mainstreaming challenges in Taiwan’s high-parity parliament to the tactical use of gender reforms by autocratic regimes for political legitimacy. We also introduce methodological and conceptual innovations, such as the role of "feminist academic critical actors" and the importance of "vernacularizing" international norms to suit local contexts. Ultimately, this issue underscores the resilience required to institutionalize gender equality amidst shifting democratic norms and varying socioeconomic conditions, offering fresh insights into how parliaments can be transformed into more inclusive workplaces and representative bodies.
humanized_abstract:
Gender equality reforms in parliaments across the world have become more diverse and more complex. This thematic issue provides insight into the dynamics of Parliament’s efforts to become more gender-just, moving away from the traditional discussion of the numbers of men and women who sit in Parliament. Instead it examines the ways in which parliamentarians and parliamentary staff commit themselves to the goal of gender equality within their own institution and ways of work. This thematic issue offers a new perspective on the gendering of Parliament, taking into account the perspectives of both international bodies looking to enforce a gender equality agenda imposed from above and civil society organisations advocating from the bottom up.

Feminist institutionalism is applied to understand the complex relationships between different types and levels of representation in various political contexts. The issue addresses a geographically broad range of political systems, taking into account both ‘ordinary’ and ‘extraordinary cases’. It covers gender mainstreaming in a representative body like Taiwan’s parliament (high degree of gender parity in the legislature), as well as the complex uses made of gender reforms by autocratic regimes aiming to enhance their legitimacy. In addition to the in-depth empirical analyses of gender-related parliamentary politics, the issue includes several contributions that present methodological and conceptual innovations relevant to feminist political science. Thus, they include a critical analysis of the activities of feminist academic critical actors and research on how international norms are vernacularized by local actors. At a more general level, the contributions in this issue reveal the resistance that must be overcome in order to secure gender-equal representation in parliament, in times when democratic frameworks and social, economic conditions are dynamically changing. Through the numerous analyses, the possibility and the ways in which a parliament can be turned into an institution for gender-egalitarian workplaces and an organization that promotes the participation of women in political affairs, are addressed in various contexts.
